message.content:
You are an adversarial production-readiness assessor for the hc-assay engine at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (dataset-agnostic, data-sovereign empirical-ML/methodology engine; optional backends import-guarded; transcripts committed + scrubbed intentionally). It has survived NINE hardening passes and CI is green — easy findings are gone. Report ONLY genuine, concrete, FALSIFIABLE production-readiness defects with a real failure mode and a file:line/symbol location; no style nits. If a dimension is clean, return few/zero findings — do NOT invent. Read real code (Read/Grep/Bash; "python -m pytest", "source .venv/bin/activate"; gh for CI). Branch harden/pass-10 (from merged main @ pass 9).

YOUR DIMENSION: privacy/PII.
Re-probe scripts/capture_transcripts.py + committed corpus for any remaining operator-identity vector or new secret/PII class (other hostnames, IPs like 192.168.x, internal URLs, tokens, the new _envparse/_local/scripts files, MERGE-POLICY.md). The handle/name/username/email/hostname classes are covered — look for something NEW.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Switched to branch 'harden/pass-10'
17b4eb5 docs(hardening): pass-9 merged (#170); open pass 10
a49b3f6 Merge pull request #170 from [REDACTED]/harden/pass-9
edbb576 docs(hardening): record pass 9 — actual-CI-execution + fix-regression each caught a HIGH

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import platform
26	import re
27	import socket
28	import sys
29	from pathlib import Path
30	
31	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
32	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
33	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
34	
35	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
36	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
37	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
38	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
39	# for the full public-release consideration.
40	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
41	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
42	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
43	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
44	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
45	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
46	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
47	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
48	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
49	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
50	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
51	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
52	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
53	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
55	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
56	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
57	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
58	]
59	
60	
61	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
62	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
63	
64	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
65	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
66	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
67	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
68	    """
69	    try:
70	        name = Path.home().name
71	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
72	        return None
73	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
74	        return None
75	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
76	
77	
78	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
79	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
80	
81	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
82	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
83	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
84	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
85	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
86	    over-redacting common words.
87	    """
88	    names: list[str] = []
89	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
90	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
91	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
92	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
93	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
94	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
95	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
96	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
97	        if v:
98	            names.append(v)
99	    try:
100	        import subprocess
101	
102	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
103	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
104	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
105	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
106	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
107	        pass
108	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
109	    seen: set[str] = set()
110	    for n in names:
111	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
112	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
113	        seen.add(n)
114	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
115	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
116	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
117	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
118	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
119	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
120	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
121	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
122	        toks = n.split()
123	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
124	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
125	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
126	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
127	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
128	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
129	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
130	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
131	        for t in toks:
132	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
133	                seen.add(t.lower())
134	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
135	    return pats
136	
137	
138	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
139	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
140	
141	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
142	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
143	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
144	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
145	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
146	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
147	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
148	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
149	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
150	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
151	    """
152	    handles: list[str] = []
153	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
154	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
155	    try:
156	        import subprocess
157	
158	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
159	        if out.returncode == 0:
160	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
161	                handles.append(m.group(1))
162	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
163	        pass
164	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
165	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
166	    expanded: list[str] = []
167	    for h in handles:
168	        expanded.append(h)
169	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
170	            expanded.append(h[3:])
171	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
172	    seen: set[str] = set()
173	    for h in expanded:
174	        key = h.lower()
175	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
176	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
177	        seen.add(key)
178	        if len(h) >= 8:
179	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
180	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
181	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
182	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
183	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
184	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
185	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
186	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
187	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
188	        else:
189	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
190	    return pats
191	
192	
193	def _hostname_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
194	    """Redactions for the operator's machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-MO/P9-PII-1).
195	
196	    The scrubber masked email/username/display-name/handle but had NO rule for the host the node
197	    runs on, so the production machine's hostname (e.g. ``[REDACTED]`` / ``[REDACTED]``) and
198	    internal ``*.local`` mDNS/namespace hosts leaked into committed transcripts — operator/infra
199	    identity. Derive the MACHINE hostname from ``socket.gethostname``/``platform.node`` (and, on
200	    macOS, ``scutil --get LocalHostName``/``ComputerName``) and redact both its bare and ``.local``
201	    forms (a machine name is distinctive). Additional internal hosts (e.g. an RDF-namespace host
202	    like ``[REDACTED]``) are supplied via the comma-separated ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS`` env and have
203	    ONLY their ``.local`` form redacted — the bare label (often a project name like ``hc-grc``)
204	    appears in legitimate paths/repo refs and must not be over-redacted.
205	    """
206	    machine: list[str] = []
207	    try:
208	        machine.append(socket.gethostname())
209	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
210	        pass
211	    try:
212	        machine.append(platform.node())
213	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
214	        pass
215	    try:
216	        import subprocess
217	
218	        for arg in ("LocalHostName", "ComputerName"):
219	            out = subprocess.run(["scutil", "--get", arg], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
220	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
221	                machine.append(out.stdout.strip())
222	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - scutil absent (non-macOS) must never break capture
223	        pass
224	    extra = [h.strip() for h in os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "").split(",") if h.strip()]
225	
226	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
227	    seen: set[str] = set()
228	
229	    def _base(name: str) -> str:
230	        n = name.strip()
231	        return n[: -len(".local")] if n.lower().endswith(".local") else n
232	
233	    for name in machine:  # machine hostname → redact BARE + .local (distinctive identity)
234	        b = _base(name)
235	        if len(b) < 4 or b.lower() in seen:
236	            continue
237	        seen.add(b.lower())
238	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
239	    for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form
240	        b = _base(name)
241	        if len(b) < 4 or ("local:" + b.lower()) in seen:
242	            continue
243	        seen.add("local:" + b.lower())
244	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"\.local\b", re.IGNORECASE))
245	    return pats
246	
247	
248	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
249	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
250	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
251	_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()
252	
253	
254	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
255	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
256	        if pat.groups == 2:
257	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
258	        elif pat.groups == 1:
259	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
260	        else:
261	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
262	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
263	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
264	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
265	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
266	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
267	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
268	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
269	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
270	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
271	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
272	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
273	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
274	    # Redact the machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-PII-1) — the production node's hostname and
275	    # internal *.local hosts that no other rule covers.
276	    for pat in _HOSTNAME_RES:
277	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
278	    return text
279	
280	
281	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
282	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
283	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
284	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
285	
286	
287	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
288	    try:
289	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
290	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
291	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
292	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
293	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
294	        #
295	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
296	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
297	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
298	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
299	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
300	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
301	        # present) is copied verbatim.
302	        if b"\x00" in raw:
303	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
304	        else:
305	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
306	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
307	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
308	        else:
309	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
310	        return {
311	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
312	            "bytes": len(data),
313	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
314	        }
315	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
316	        return None
317	
318	
319	def main() -> int:
320	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
321	    if not src.is_dir():
322	        return 0
323	    session = _active_session(src)
324	    if session is None:
325	        return 0
326	    sid = session.stem
327	    captured: list[dict] = []
328	
329	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
330	    if rec:
331	        captured.append(rec)
332	
333	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
334	    subtree = src / sid
335	    if subtree.is_dir():
336	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
337	            if f.is_file():
338	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
339	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
340	                if rec:
341	                    captured.append(rec)
342	
343	    try:
344	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
345	        MANIFEST.write_text(
346	            json.dumps(
347	                {
348	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
349	                    "active_session": sid,
350	                    "n_files": len(captured),
351	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
352	                    "files": captured,
353	                },
354	                indent=2,
355	            )
356	            + "\n",
357	            encoding="utf-8",
358	        )
359	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
360	        return 0
361	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
362	    return 0
363	
364	
365	if __name__ == "__main__":
366	    sys.exit(main())
367

content.text: Let me check MERGE-POLICY.md and the _local/_envparse source files for PII, plus look at the Peplink/router context which is a second leaked infra identifier.

content.content:
1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def is_local_socket_path(host: str | None) -> bool:
42	    """True for a libpq Unix-domain-socket host — a leading-'/' absolute directory (#K-SEC-1).
43	
44	    libpq accepts ``host=/var/run/postgresql`` (or the query form ``?host=/tmp``) to connect over
45	    a local Unix-domain socket — a connection that NEVER touches the network, so it is strictly
46	    more data-sovereign than the TCP loopback the guard already permits. A single leading '/' is a
47	    local path; a Windows UNC prefix (two leading separators, e.g. ``//server`` or ``/\\server``)
48	    resolves to a remote SMB share and must still be rejected.
49	    """
50	    if not host:
51	        return False
52	    h = host.strip()
53	    # A comma means libpq MULTI-HOST (e.g. "/tmp,evil.com") — never a single socket dir. Reject so
54	    # the leading-'/' check can't wave through an off-box host hidden after the comma (#K-SEC-9-1).
55	    # Callers split multi-host and validate each element; a leaf reaching here must be one host.
56	    if "," in h:
57	        return False
58	    return h.startswith("/") and not h.startswith("//") and not h.startswith("/\\")
59	
60	
61	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
62	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
63	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
64	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
65	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
66	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
67	            f"from {url!r}"
68	        )
69	    return url
70	
71	
72	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
73	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
74	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
75	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
76	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
77	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
78	        )
79	    return host
80	
81	
82	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
83	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
84	
85	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
86	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
87	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
88	
89	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
90	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
91	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
92	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
93	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
94	    """
95	    if "@" in netloc:
96	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
97	    hosts: list[str] = []
98	    for part in netloc.split(","):
99	        p = part.strip()
100	        if not p:
101	            continue
102	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
103	            end = p.find("]")
104	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
105	        else:
106	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
107	    return hosts
108	
109	
110	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
111	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
112	
113	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
114	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
115	    reports, since the two can disagree:
116	
117	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
118	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
119	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
120	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
121	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
122	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
123	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
124	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
125	
126	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
127	    """
128	    try:
129	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
130	        netloc = parsed.netloc
131	        query = parsed.query
132	    except ValueError:
133	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
134	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
135	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
136	        ) from None
137	
138	    if netloc:
139	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
140	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
141	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
142	        if "\\" in netloc:
143	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
144	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
145	            )
146	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
147	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
148	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
149	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
150	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
151	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
152	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
153	                )
154	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
155	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
156	                # libpq allows a COMMA-SEPARATED multi-host value; split and validate EVERY element
157	                # (#K-SEC-9-1) — else "/tmp,evil.com" slipped through because the whole string
158	                # looked like a socket path and the off-box host after the comma was never checked.
159	                for h in (p.strip() for p in val.split(",")):
160	                    if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
161	                        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
162	                            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
163	                            f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
164	                        )
165	        return uri
166	
167	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
168	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
169	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
170	                raw = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
171	                # Split libpq's comma-separated multi-host and validate each element (#K-SEC-9-1).
172	                for h in (p.strip() for p in raw.split(",")):
173	                    if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
174	                        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
175	                            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
176	                            f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
177	                        )
178	        return uri
179	
180	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
181	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
182	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
183	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
184	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
185	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
186	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
187	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
188	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
189	        )
190	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
191

content.text:
The `_local.py`/`_envparse.py`/`MERGE-POLICY.md` are clean. The finding is solid and concentrated: operator network-topology PII (LAN IP, gateway IP, ISP/router vendor) leaked via shell-command outputs (`lsof -i`, `route get`, `ifconfig`) in committed transcripts, with no scrubber coverage.

Let me do one final check to scope the fix correctly: confirm the leaked IPs are stable identifiers and count total exposure, and verify the public peer IP 160.79.104.10 (which the established-connections show) isn't also operator-revealing.

content.text:
I have a confirmed, falsifiable finding. Let me finalize.

The finding: the transcript scrubber (`scripts/capture_transcripts.py`) has redaction rules for email, username, display name, GitHub handle, and hostname — but NO rule for IP addresses or network topology. The operator's real private LAN IP (`[REDACTED]`, which `ipconfig getifaddr en0` confirms is this exact machine's current address), gateway IP (`[REDACTED]`), interface (`en1`), and ISP/router vendor ("Peplink cellular") leaked into committed transcripts via the output of routine diagnostic shell commands (`lsof -i` connection table, `route get` default-route dump, `ifconfig`). The hostname on those same lines IS redacted, proving the scrubber ran and simply has no IP coverage.

findings.title: Operator private LAN/gateway IP + interface + ISP vendor leak into committed transcripts — scrubber has no IP/network-topology rule
findings.location: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:40-58 (_SECRET_PATTERNS) and :193-245 (_hostname_patterns); leaked corpus e.g. transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d.jsonl and .../tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt
findings.claim: The scrubber redacts email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle/hostname but has NO rule for IP addresses or network topology. The operator's real machine private LAN IP [REDACTED], the LAN gateway [REDACTED], the active interface en1, and the ISP/router vendor 'Peplink cellular' leaked into committed transcripts verbatim via the output of routine diagnostic shell commands (lsof -i connection table 'TCP [REDACTED]:49227->160.79.104.10:443 (ESTABLISHED)', `route get default` dump 'gateway: [REDACTED] / interface: en1', and ifconfig). [REDACTED] is verifiably THIS node's current address: `ipconfig getifaddr en0` returns exactly [REDACTED]. These are a distinct PII class from the host/name/email/handle classes prior passes covered.
findings.why_real: Falsifiable and verified: (1) `grep -niE 'ip|192|168|inet|lsof|netstat|route|gateway' scripts/capture_transcripts.py` matches only docstring/path text — there is literally no IP-redaction code path. (2) `ipconfig getifaddr en0` on this node returns [REDACTED], and `ifconfig | grep 'inet 192.168'` confirms [REDACTED] — so the committed value is the operator's live LAN address, not a test fixture. (3) The leaking file tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt contains 20 '[REDACTED]' markers (hostname/username WERE scrubbed on the same lsof lines) while the IP survives — proving the scrubber ran and simply lacks coverage, not that the file was skipped. (4) The .50.x/.99.x test-fixture IPs in src ([REDACTED], [REDACTED]) are intentional ADR-0003 non-loopback examples and are NOT the concern; the concern is the en0-confirmed real-host triple {LAN IP, gateway, interface} plus the named ISP/router brand. A LAN IP + gateway + ISP-equipment vendor narrows the operator's physical network and, combined with the established-connection peer IPs, is operator/infrastructure de-anonymization data in a corpus the README explicitly intends to publish.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a network-topology redaction pass to _scrub. Minimum: derive the node's own non-loopback IPv4/IPv6 addresses at runtime (e.g. socket.getaddrinfo(socket.gethostname(), None) and/or psutil/`ipconfig getifaddr`/`ifconfig` parsing) plus the default-gateway IP, and redact those exact literals (they are stable, unambiguous identifiers — analogous to the existing hostname rule). Provide an ASSAY_SCRUB_IPS env (comma-separated) for the gateway and any other infra IPs that aren't bound to a local interface. Also add the router/ISP vendor token(s) (e.g. 'Peplink') to the ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES-style handling so the equipment brand is masked. Do NOT blanket-redact all RFC1918 IPs — that would destroy the legitimate ADR-0003 loopback/non-loopback test fixtures (127.0.0.1, [REDACTED], [REDACTED]) which are intended example values; redact only the runtime-derived operator-specific literals. Then re-run capture over the existing corpus to scrub the already-committed leaks.

content.text:
I found one genuine, falsifiable privacy/PII defect in the privacy dimension.

## Finding: Operator network-topology PII leak (HIGH)

**Location:** `scripts/capture_transcripts.py` — the scrubber's `_SECRET_PATTERNS` (lines 40-58) and `_hostname_patterns` (193-245) cover email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle/hostname but have **no IP or network-topology rule**.

**The leak:** The operator's real machine private LAN IP `[REDACTED]`, LAN gateway `[REDACTED]`, active interface `en1`, and ISP/router vendor "Peplink cellular" are committed verbatim in `transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-…jsonl` and `.../tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt`, leaked via routine diagnostic command output (`lsof -i` connection table, `route get default`, `ifconfig`).

**Why it's real (verified, not invented):**
- `ipconfig getifaddr en0` on this node returns exactly `[REDACTED]` — the committed value is the live operator LAN address, not a test fixture.
- The leaking file has 20 `[REDACTED]` markers on the same `lsof` lines (hostname/username were scrubbed) while the IP survives — proving the scrubber ran and simply lacks coverage.
- `grep` for any IP/route/lsof term in the scrubber matches only docstring text; there is no IP code path.

**Important scoping (avoids a false fix):** The `[REDACTED]` / `[REDACTED]` values in `src/` are intentional ADR-0003 non-loopback test fixtures and must NOT be redacted. The fix must target only runtime-derived operator-specific literals (own interface IPs + gateway + named vendor), not all RFC1918 addresses.

The other NEW probe targets were clean: `src/assay_engine/_local.py`, `src/assay_engine/_envparse.py`, and `docs/MERGE-POLICY.md` contain no PII.
